nativeRange.habitat: The Bristletooth Tomini Tang inhabits sheltered coastal reefs with steep, coral-rich drop-offs, typically found between 3 and 25 m depth, though it has been recorded as shallow as the surface and as deep as 45 m. It is generally observed singly or in small groups, grazing surface algal films and microfauna from hard substrate.

decorPreferences: live rock with biofilm and algal turf for grazing
rockwork crevices and overhangs for shelter
long horizontal footprint preferred over tall depth
sand bed for natural grazing behaviour

diet.notes: The Tomini Tang uses individually movable, bristle-like teeth to graze surface algal films, detritus, and small microfauna from live rock. Marine-based seaweed such as dried nori and spirulina-rich herbivore preparations are generally advised at least three times per week. It has been reported to accept algae-based flakes and pellets, frozen vegetable preparations, and small meaty items like mysis shrimp alongside tankmates. Vitamin-supplemented diets are commonly recommended to reduce the risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion, a condition in which pores along the head and lateral line become visibly pitted — often associated with poor water quality or nutritional deficiency in tang-family fish.

goodWith: non-Ctenochaetus tangs (with caution and ample space)
avoidWith: similarly shaped or similarly coloured surgeonfish

imageCandidates.notes: Brian Gratwicke, 3456x2304. Tack-sharp full lateral view; vivid golden-orange dorsal fin tip, white caudal fin, golden flecks and blue eye-ring clearly shown. Striking, best-focused pick.
Rufus46, 3860x3010. Aquarium shot of dark-brown adult with golden dorsal/anal fins and white tail, matching the adult colour description; slight water particulate but sharp.
Rickard Zerpe, 3179x2384. In-situ reef shot, whole fish, sharp; brown body with yellow dorsal/anal fin tips and pale caudal fin. Adds natural-habitat variety.

summary: A small Western Pacific bristletooth surgeonfish with a dark brown body, golden-yellow dorsal and anal fin tips, and a white caudal fin. Generally considered one of the more peaceable and beginner-friendly tangs, well suited to medium-sized reef systems.
careNotes:
The Bristletooth Tomini Tang (Ctenochaetus tominiensis) is one of the smaller members of the bristletooth surgeonfish genus, reaching around 15 to 17 cm as an adult. Native to sheltered reefs of the Western Pacific — including Indonesia, the Philippines, Papua New Guinea, the northern Great Barrier Reef, Solomon Islands, Palau, Vanuatu, Fiji, and Tonga — it is sometimes sold under the trade name 'Flame Fin Tomini Tang,' a nod to its golden-orange fin tips.

The Tomini is widely regarded as one of the most manageable tangs for medium-sized reef systems and is commonly recommended for aquarists keeping a tang for the first time. A tank of around 300 L with a long horizontal footprint is generally advised, since bristletooth tangs are active grazers throughout the day and do best with ample open swimming space alongside extensive live rock that supports natural grazing routes.

Water parameters should be stable and well-oxygenated within standard reef ranges: specific gravity 1.022–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, and alkalinity 8–12 dKH.

Diet should lean heavily on marine algae. Dried seaweed such as nori and spirulina-based herbivore foods are generally advised at least three times per week. Vitamin-rich diets are commonly recommended to help prevent Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), a condition in which pores along the head and the sensory line running down the body become visibly pitted — one that tang-family fish are reported to develop under poor water quality or nutritional deficiency.

The Tomini is generally considered reef-safe. It is best kept as the sole representative of the bristletooth genus per tank, as conspecific aggression toward other Ctenochaetus and similarly shaped tangs has been reported; aggression toward unrelated reef fish tends to be low relative to other tang species. Juveniles display a tan body with yellow, blue, and white highlights; adults develop a more uniform dark-brown body with golden-yellow dorsal and anal fin tips, golden flecks above the eyes, and a white-to-pale-blue tail.

Like all members of the surgeonfish family, the Tomini Tang carries sharp blades at the base of the tail (the caudal peduncle) — the feature that gives the group its common name 'surgeonfish.' These blades are not venomous, but they can inflict lacerations on handlers and tankmates during conflicts; care should be taken when netting or transferring the fish.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Bristletooth Tomini Tang has not been documented. Like most surgeonfish, the species is presumed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild, releasing eggs and sperm into open water where the larvae develop as plankton. Spawning in home aquaria is considered highly unlikely given typical tank sizes and the difficulty of establishing compatible pairs.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records have been documented. Like most surgeonfish, the species is presumed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild; spawning in a home aquarium is considered highly unlikely.
